schools.summary: Gill: Jacob called his sons, who were near at hand or whom he sent for, to tell them what should befall them in the latter days; the witnesses note Jacob calling his sons together to tell them what should befall them in the last days — the solemn opening of the patriarch's prophetic blessing, the dying father gathering his sons to declare their future (Jacob, near death, summoning his sons for a prophetic word: 'Gather yourselves together, that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days'; the gathering, that Jacob 'called unto his sons' and bade them 'gather... together,' that all the twelve might hear at once the words he was about to speak; the prophetic character of what follows, 'that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days,' that Jacob, by the Spirit of God, would foretell the future of each son and his tribe, even to 'the last days,' reaching down through the generations and, in Judah's portion, to the days of Messiah; that this was no mere fatherly counsel but a prophecy, the aged patriarch made a prophet in his dying hour; the solemnity of a deathbed blessing, the last and weightiest words of the father of the tribes).

themes: And Jacob called unto his sons, and said, Gather yourselves together
that I may tell you that which shall befall you in the last days
the solemn opening of the patriarch's prophetic blessing
the dying patriarch made a prophet, reaching to the days of Messiah

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the repeated summons, Gather yourselves together and hear, ye sons of Jacob, and hearken unto Israel your father; Gill: this is repeated to hasten them and to suggest he had something of importance to make known, which he chose to do when they were together; the witnesses note Jacob repeating the call to gather and hear, as sons of Jacob, and to hearken to Israel their father — the urgent and twofold summons to attend, marking the weight of the coming words (Jacob doubling his call to fix their attention: 'Gather yourselves together, and hear, ye sons of Jacob; and hearken unto Israel your father'; the repetition, that Jacob says 'gather yourselves together' again, to hasten them and to mark that he had a weighty thing to declare, fit to be heard by all at once; the double word for attention, 'hear... and hearken,' pressing them to give earnest heed to what their dying father should say; the two names the commentators mark, 'ye sons of Jacob' and 'Israel your father,' the natural name and the covenant name, that they should hear both as the children of Jacob and as the heirs of the Israel of God, to whom the promises belonged; the solemnity of the charge, fitting words that were prophecy and last testament both).

themes: Gather yourselves together, and hear, ye sons of Jacob
and hearken unto Israel your father
Jacob's urgent twofold summons to earnest attention
naming them both sons of Jacob and heirs of Israel, the covenant name

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob addressed Reuben first, owned him as his firstborn, did not cashier him from his family nor disinherit him, but acknowledged his natural pre-eminence, the beginning of his strength, to whom belonged the dignity and power of the firstborn; the witnesses note Jacob owning Reuben as his firstborn, his might and the beginning of his strength, the excellency of dignity and of power — the acknowledgment of Reuben's natural birthright dignity before the sentence that follows (Jacob first setting out what Reuben was by birth: 'Reuben, thou art my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength, the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power'; the honour owned, that Reuben was Jacob's 'firstborn,' 'the beginning of my strength,' the first fruit of his manhood, to whom by birth belonged 'the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power,' the firstborn's threefold prerogative of the double portion, the priesthood, and the rule in the family; that Jacob does not deny Reuben his natural place as eldest, owning him fully and tenderly; that this acknowledgment makes the coming sentence the more solemn, that one born to such dignity should forfeit it; the setting-up of the height from which Reuben fell).

themes: Reuben, thou art my firstborn, my might, and the beginning of my strength
the excellency of dignity, and the excellency of power
Jacob owning Reuben's natural birthright dignity
the firstborn's threefold prerogative, the height from which he fell

schools.summary: Gill: this is not to be understood of mere levity of mind but of the impetuous force of his lust breaking forth like water, carrying him into sin; Reuben should not excel, his tribe never rising to greatness; the witnesses note Jacob declaring Reuben unstable as water and that he should not excel, because he defiled his father's bed — the solemn sentence of forfeited pre-eminence for Reuben's grievous sin (Jacob pronouncing the loss of the birthright: 'Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel; because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it'; the figure, 'Unstable as water,' that Reuben was like water, which boils up and overflows but has no firmness, his passions ungoverned, breaking forth in the impetuous lust that carried him into sin; the sentence, 'thou shalt not excel,' that, for all his birthright dignity, Reuben should not have the pre-eminence, his tribe never rising to greatness or rule in Israel; the cause, 'because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it,' the grievous sin of Reuben with Bilhah, by which he dishonoured his father and forfeited his place; the justice of God, that sin loses what nature gave, the firstborn's honour passing from Reuben, the rule to Judah, the priesthood to Levi, the double portion to Joseph).

debates.question: Why does Reuben forfeit the birthright?

sides.position: For his grievous sin in defiling his father's bed with Bilhah (Genesis 35:22) — his ungoverned passion, 'unstable as water,' losing the firstborn's pre-eminence; the honour passing to Judah (rule), Levi (priesthood), and Joseph (double portion)

themes: Unstable as water, thou shalt not excel
because thou wentest up to thy father's bed; then defiledst thou it
the sentence on Reuben, his ungoverned passion forfeiting the birthright
the justice of God, that sin loses what nature gave

schools.summary: Gill: they are called brethren not merely as being so by father and mother, for so were others, but because they were of like tempers, dispositions, and conduct, alike in their fierce and cruel disposition; the witnesses note Jacob calling Simeon and Levi brethren, with instruments of cruelty in their habitations — the joining of the two for their shared fierce and cruel temper, shown at Shechem (Jacob marking them as one in their violence: 'Simeon and Levi are brethren; instruments of cruelty are in their habitations'; the sense of 'brethren' here, not the natural tie only, for all twelve were brethren, but that Simeon and Levi were 'brethren' in temper, alike in their fierce, cruel, and revengeful disposition, confederate in evil; the charge, 'instruments of cruelty are in their habitations,' that their swords, or their counsels and devices, were instruments of cruelty, pointing back to the bloody deed at Shechem, where they slew all the males in revenge for their sister Dinah; that Jacob, even on his deathbed, would not pass over so great a sin but marks it for the warning of their tribes; the difference of this from a blessing, that for these two the father's word was reproof and sentence).

themes: Simeon and Levi are brethren
instruments of cruelty are in their habitations
Jacob joining Simeon and Levi as brethren in fierce, cruel temper
their swords instruments of cruelty, pointing to the bloody deed at Shechem

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob said this as abhorring the wicked counsel they had taken of slaying the Shechemites, and lest any should think he had a hand in it; the witnesses note Jacob praying that his soul come not into their secret nor his honour be joined to their assembly, for in their anger they slew a man and in their selfwill digged down a wall — the patriarch's solemn disavowal of their bloody deed, clearing himself of all share in it (Jacob abhorring and renouncing their counsel: 'O my soul, come not thou into their secret... for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall'; the earnest disclaiming, 'O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou united,' that Jacob, abhorring their 'secret,' their cruel counsel and conspiracy at Shechem, prays that he had no part in it, neither his 'soul' nor his 'honour' joined to so bloody a deed; the charge stated plainly, 'in their anger they slew a man,' the men of Shechem, 'and in their selfwill they digged down a wall,' or houghed oxen, broke down the city, a deed of fierce anger and stubborn self-will; that Jacob, far from owning the deed, here solemnly clears himself before God and his sons of any share in their cruelty; the abhorrence of a godly man for bloody violence done even in a kinsman's cause).

themes: O my soul, come not thou into their secret; unto their assembly, mine honour, be not thou united
for in their anger they slew a man, and in their selfwill they digged down a wall
Jacob's solemn disavowal of the bloody deed at Shechem
the abhorrence of a godly man for fierce violence in a kinsman's cause

schools.summary: Gill: their anger was sinful in its nature, criminal and detestable, strong, fierce, and furious in its operation, and so justly cursed, not the persons but the sin; the witnesses note Jacob cursing their fierce anger and cruel wrath, and declaring he would divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel — the sentence on their sin and its strange fulfilment in the scattering of the two tribes (Jacob cursing the sin and foretelling the punishment: 'Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel: I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel'; the careful aim of the curse, 'Cursed be their anger... and their wrath,' that Jacob curses not the persons of his sons but their 'anger' and 'wrath,' the sinful passion itself, 'fierce' and 'cruel,' justly accursed; the sentence, 'I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel,' that the two tribes should be scattered and not have a settled, undivided inheritance of their own; the remarkable fulfilment the commentators trace, that Simeon's portion lay scattered within Judah's border and the tribe dwindled, while Levi received no land at all but was dispersed in cities throughout Israel, yet Levi's scattering, by God's grace, was turned to honour in the priesthood; the truth that God can make even a curse the means of mercy, as in Levi).

debates.question: How was the scattering of Simeon and Levi fulfilled?

sides.position: Simeon's portion lay scattered within Judah's border and the tribe dwindled; Levi received no land but was dispersed in cities throughout Israel — yet Levi's scattering, by God's grace, was turned to honour in the priesthood

themes: Cursed be their anger, for it was fierce; and their wrath, for it was cruel
I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel
Jacob cursing the sin, not the persons, and foretelling the scattering
fulfilled in Simeon's dwindling and Levi's dispersal, yet Levi's turned to honour

schools.summary: Gill: Judah's name signifies praise, given by his mother whose heart was filled with praises to God; he should be praised by his brethren for his virtues, his tribe famous for heroes such as David, and especially his famous seed the Messiah, of whom he was a type; the witnesses note Judah being praised by his brethren, his hand on the neck of his enemies, and his brethren bowing before him — the turn from sentence to blessing, the pre-eminence given to Judah and pointing to Christ (Jacob exalting Judah above his brethren: 'Judah, thou art he whom thy brethren shall praise: thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies; thy father's children shall bow down before thee'; the play on the name, 'Judah' meaning 'praise,' that he should be 'he whom thy brethren shall praise,' honoured and esteemed for his virtues; the victory promised, 'thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies,' that Judah should subdue his foes in conquest; the rule given, 'thy father's children shall bow down before thee,' that the other tribes should yield him pre-eminence and homage; the great type the commentators mark, that all this points beyond the tribe to Christ, the Lion of Judah, whom his brethren praise, who subdues his enemies and before whom every knee shall bow).

debates.question: Whom does the exaltation of Judah ultimately point to?

sides.position: Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah — whom his brethren praise, who subdues his enemies, and before whom every knee shall bow; Judah and his pre-eminence are a type of the Messiah who springs from his line

themes: Judah, thou art he whom thy brethren shall praise; thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies
thy father's children shall bow down before thee
Judah exalted above his brethren, praised, victorious, given rule
a type of Christ, the Lion of Judah, before whom every knee shall bow

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob compares Judah to a young lion, gone up from the prey, lying down like a lion, like a lioness whom none can rouse; Gill: he was comparable to a young lion for his strength, courage, and generosity; the witnesses note Judah likened to a lion's whelp, gone up from the prey, couched as a lion whom none dare rouse — the figure of the lion that marks Judah's strength and dominion, and points to Christ the Lion of Judah (Jacob setting forth Judah's lion-like majesty: 'Judah is a lion's whelp: from the prey, my son, thou art gone up: he stooped down, he couched as a lion... who shall rouse him up?'; the threefold image, the 'lion's whelp,' the young lion growing in strength, the lion 'gone up from the prey,' returned victorious from the hunt, and the lion that has 'couched' or 'lain down' in such majesty and might that none dare 'rouse him up'; that this figures Judah's growth from a young tribe to a strong and ruling one, courageous, victorious, and secure in his strength; the great Messianic reference the commentators draw, that the 'Lion of the tribe of Judah' is Christ himself, strong to conquer, who, having gone up from the prey of his finished work, reigns in unassailable majesty; the dignity of the figure, fit for both the tribe and its great Son).

debates.question: What does the lion figure of Judah point to?

sides.position: Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5) — strong to conquer, victorious from the prey of his finished work, reigning in unassailable majesty; the lion figures both the tribe's strength and dominion and its great Son

themes: Judah is a lion's whelp: from the prey, my son, thou art gone up
he couched as a lion, and as an old lion; who shall rouse him up?
Judah the lion, figuring strength and dominion
pointing to the Lion of the tribe of Judah, Christ reigning in majesty

schools.summary: Gill: the sceptre signifies dominion, power, and authority, not the bare continuance of the tribe, for Benjamin and Levi also continued; it should not depart from Judah until Shiloh come, that is, the Messiah, to whom the gathering of the people should be; the witnesses note Jacob foretelling that the sceptre and lawgiver should not depart from Judah until Shiloh come, and unto him the gathering of the people — the supreme Messianic prophecy of the chapter, the rule abiding in Judah until Christ (Jacob speaking the great word of promise: 'The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come; and unto him shall the gathering of the people be'; the meaning of 'the sceptre' and 'lawgiver,' not the bare continuance of the tribe but 'dominion, power, and authority,' that rule and government should belong to Judah and not depart until a set time; the great term 'Shiloh,' which the commentators take for the Messiah, whether read as 'he whose right it is,' 'the peaceable one,' or 'the sent,' that the sceptre should abide in Judah 'until Shiloh come,' until the coming of Christ who sprang from Judah; the fulfilment, that Judah held a distinct tribal and ruling standing until the time of Christ, and that with his coming the old polity passed; the gathering, 'unto him shall the gathering of the people be,' that to Shiloh the nations should be gathered, the Gentiles brought in).

debates.question: Who is 'Shiloh,' and what is the meaning of the prophecy?

sides.position: The Messiah, Christ, who sprang from Judah — the commentators read 'Shiloh' as 'he whose right it is,' 'the peaceable one,' or 'the sent'; dominion abides in Judah until his coming, and to him ('the gathering of the people') the nations and Gentiles are gathered

themes: The sceptre shall not depart from Judah, nor a lawgiver from between his feet, until Shiloh come
and unto him shall the gathering of the people be
the supreme Messianic prophecy, rule abiding in Judah until Shiloh the Messiah
to him the gathering of the people, the Gentiles brought in

schools.summary: Gill: this may signify that vines should grow in such plenty in Judah's land that a man might bind his ass to a vine, Judah's land as Engedi being famous for vines, or it may be applied to Shiloh the Messiah; the witnesses note the binding of the foal to the vine and the washing of garments in wine and the blood of grapes — the figures of the rich plenty of Judah's land, applied also to Christ and his blood (Jacob painting abundance under the vine and the wine: 'Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine; he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes'; the first figure, 'binding his foal unto the vine... unto the choice vine,' that Judah's land should so abound in vines, large and strong, that one might tie an ass to a vine as to a post and not grieve if it ate of so plentiful a growth, a picture of great fruitfulness; the second figure, 'he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes,' the grapes so plentiful that wine should be as common as water for washing, a hyperbole of abundance; the Messianic application the commentators draw, that 'the blood of grapes' points to the blood of Christ, the true Vine, who treads the winepress and washes his garments in blood; the richness fit for both the fruitful tribe and its great Son).

themes: Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass's colt unto the choice vine
he washed his garments in wine, and his clothes in the blood of grapes
the figures of Judah's rich plenty, vines and wine in abundance
the Messianic glance, the blood of grapes pointing to the blood of Christ, the true Vine

schools.summary: Gill: this signifies not the intemperance of the tribe but the goodness and generosity of their wine, that Judah's land should abound in wine and milk, and (applied to Messiah) may denote the joy that sparkled in his eyes or his comeliness; the witnesses note the eyes red with wine and the teeth white with milk — the closing figure of Judah's abundance of wine and milk, with a Messianic glance (Jacob completing the picture of plenty: 'His eyes shall be red with wine, and his teeth white with milk'; the meaning, not the 'intemperance of this tribe' but 'the goodness and generosity of their wine,' that Judah's land should so abound in wine and milk that the people should be ruddy and well-favoured, the eyes bright with the richness of wine and the teeth white with milk, a picture of health, plenty, and prosperity; that wine and milk together figure the fulness of the land, the vineyards and the pastures both yielding abundantly; the Messianic application the commentators offer, that as applied to Christ, the antitype of Judah, the figures may denote his comeliness, or the joy and the saving fulness found in him, who gives the wine and milk of the gospel freely; the richness of blessing that closes Judah's portion).

themes: His eyes shall be red with wine
and his teeth white with milk
the closing figure of Judah's abundance of wine and milk
a Messianic glance at the saving fulness in Christ, freely given
